definition: Free distribution of cryptocurrency tokens to a group of users. Often used to launch tokens or reward community members.
A DEX mechanism where users trade against a liquidity pool rather than an order book. Prices determined by smart contract formula.
The annual return on an investment, accounting for compound interest. Shows true earning potential vs simple interest (APR).
Profiting from price differences of the same asset across different markets. Requires buying low in one market and selling high in another.
Professional review of smart contract code to identify vulnerabilities and security issues before deployment.
Distributed ledger technology using cryptography. Records transactions in linked blocks verified by network participants.
Transferring assets from one blockchain to another. Enables cross-chain interoperability but introduces smart contract risk.
The protocol by which network participants agree on the current state. Proof of Work and Proof of Stake are common examples.
Organization governed by smart contracts and token holders voting on proposals. No central authority.
Financial protocols and applications built on blockchain. Replaces traditional finance intermediaries with smart contracts.
Peer-to-peer trading protocol for swapping tokens without central intermediary. Examples: Uniswap, Curve, Balancer.
Application built on blockchain backend. Frontend looks like normal apps but backend uses smart contracts.
The rate at which new tokens are created and distributed in a protocol. Critical to token economics and inflation.
Ethereum token standard. Defines how fungible tokens (like currency) work and interact with other contracts.
Ethereum standard for non-fungible tokens. Each token is unique and non-interchangeable.
The computational engine that executes smart contracts on Ethereum and compatible chains. Bytecode sandbox.
Unit measuring computational cost on blockchain. Higher complexity = higher gas cost. Paid in native token.
Token that gives holders voting rights on protocol changes. Critical for DAOs and decentralized decisions.
Loss liquidity providers face when depositing in AMMs. Occurs when asset prices change significantly from deposit time.
Smart contract holding equal value of token pairs. Users trade against the pool; liquidity providers earn fees.
Profit miners/validators can extract by reordering transactions. Includes front-running and sandwich attacks.
Creation of new tokens or NFTs on the blockchain. Increases total supply and transfers new asset to user.
Unique digital asset on blockchain. Each NFT has distinct properties and cannot be directly exchanged for another.
Smart contracts that feed external real-world data onto the blockchain. Critical for DeFi but introduce trust assumptions.
Consensus mechanism where validators are chosen based on stake held. More energy efficient than Proof of Work.
Consensus mechanism requiring computational work to validate blocks. Used by Bitcoin. Energy intensive but very secure.
Core smart contract system defining how a blockchain application works. The 'rules of the game'.
Smart contract vulnerability where function calls back into themselves before state updates complete. Causes fund theft.
Percentage of sale price creator receives each time NFT is resold. Built into smart contract.
Penalty where validator loses stake for malicious behavior or downtime. Incentivizes honest participation in Proof of Stake.
Self-executing code deployed on blockchain. Runs automatically when conditions are met. No intermediary needed.
Difference between expected and actual price when executing large trades. Higher slippage = worse prices.
Depositing tokens to secure network and earn rewards. Validator in Proof of Stake systems.
Design of token supply, distribution, and incentives. Critical to project success and sustainability.
Aggregate USD value of all assets in a protocol. Key metric for DeFi protocol size and success.
Schedule releasing tokens over time. Prevents early dumping and aligns incentives over long term.
Software managing cryptographic keys. Stores, sends, and receives cryptocurrency. Examples: MetaMask, Ledger, Phantom.
Depositing crypto to earn returns. Often through liquidity provision or staking in DeFi protocols.
